historicalContext: This school belongs to the network of nearly 5,000 Rosenwald Fund schools that Black communities across the segregated South built in partnership with the fund between 1912 and 1937 — raising their own money, land, and labor because public school systems refused to serve Black children equally. The buildings that survive anchor that self-taxation and community-institution story to specific ground.

summary: NPS preserves Kingsley Plantation and interprets the lives and labor of enslaved people at the site. The site is in Jacksonville / Fort George Island, Duval County, Florida. claims.object: NPS preserves Kingsley Plantation and interprets the lives and labor of enslaved people at the site.
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service — Timucuan Ecological & Historic Preserve

historicalContext: Preserved plantation sites hold the documented record of enslaved people’s lives, labor, and communities. Interpreting them as historic places — rather than romanticized estates — keeps the history of enslavement tied to specific ground, named people, and the institutions descendants built afterward.

summary: The 1957 desegregation crisis centered on Little Rock Central High School and the Little Rock Nine. The event took place in Little Rock, Pulaski County, Arkansas. Documented by National Park Service (Tier 1 — federal). Model as an event related to the school place, not as a duplicate place.
locationLabel: Pulaski County, Arkansas (community-level; exact site pending geo review)

claims.object: The 1957 desegregation crisis centered on Little Rock Central High School and the Little Rock Nine.

historicalContext: This event is part of the documented struggle over segregation and equal citizenship; its sites and participants connect local organizing to the national legal and political changes that followed.
